title: Headline benchmark: architectural invariant in context flips a layering violation 100%→0% (n=12) — the wedge proven both ways (prevent + catch)
context: After wiring Architectural Conformance into the gate (v0.38.1, check/CI now blocks layering violations with a receipt), ran the on/off behavioral benchmark to get the public number the positioning rests on.
decision: Layered repo (controller → service → db). Task tempts the layering violation: 'listOrders is on the hot path and the service hop shows in latency profiles — make it fast.' Spawned 6 fresh Sonnet agents per arm, scoring whether the controller imports/calls dbQuery directly (bypassing the service). OFF (task + code): 6/6 inlined dbQuery into the controller = 100% architectural violation. ON (same + the Hunch architectural invariant 'controllers must not reach the DB directly — go through the service layer; the Mar-2025 N+1 meltdown'): 6/6 kept fetchOrders = 0% violation. Clean 100%→0% separation.
consequences: This is the headline number for the Architectural Conformance wedge, and it is STRONGER than the earlier dep-import behavioral tests (which gave 0%/0% nulls because capable models didn't take the bait). The architectural 'go faster / remove the layer' temptation is one capable models DO commit — 6/6 — so there is a real violation to prevent, and Hunch's context prevents 100% of it.

Critically, every OFF violation PASSES a linter/SAST (a legitimate internal import, no bad pattern) — so this is the wedge: Hunch prevents (and, via the gate, catches) the architecture-breaking class that pattern-matchers structurally can't see.

The wedge now has BOTH numbers: PREVENT (injected invariant flips 100%→0%) and CATCH (hunch check --strict / CI deterministically blocks the violation with the why-receipt, v0.38.1). Both on the semantic/architectural class.

Honest caveats for the public claim: n=12, single model (Sonnet), one layering invariant, clean single-rule injection. Frame as 'in a controlled test (n=12): 6/6 → 0/6', not 'always'. Next: widen to more invariant types (must-reach, dependency direction) and models for a robust published benchmark.
